setup.goal: make sure you get the right change back
setup.context: At the ice cream truck with a five dollar bill

root.narration: You buy an ice cream bar for three dollars and hand the ice cream man a five dollar bill. He gives you back one dollar. You know five minus three equals two, so you should get two dollars back. What do you do?

choices.text: Politely say excuse me, I think I should get two dollars back, not one
Take the one dollar and walk away because you do not want to cause trouble
You are not sure how change works so you just take whatever he gives you

outcome.headline: You caught the mistake!
outcome.explanation: The ice cream man checked and said oops, you are right! He gave you the extra dollar. Mistakes happen, and knowing your math helped you get the right amount back.
outcome.whatToRemember: Always count your change. Subtract the price from what you paid to know how much you should get back. It is okay to politely speak up if something is wrong.

keyTerms.definition: The money you get back when you pay more than something costs. Change equals what you paid minus the price.

outcome.headline: You lost a dollar!
outcome.explanation: You knew something was wrong but did not say anything. That is one less dollar in your pocket. It is always okay to politely ask someone to double check.
outcome.whatToRemember: Speaking up politely is not rude. If you know the math is wrong, say something! You are protecting your own money.

outcome.headline: You got shortchanged!
outcome.explanation: Because you did not know how to figure out the right change, you lost a dollar and did not even realize it. Learning to subtract prices from payments is an important skill.
outcome.whatToRemember: Practice subtraction with money. If something costs three dollars and you pay five dollars, you should get five minus three equals two dollars back.
